lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Now all the youth of England are on fire,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And silken dalliance in the wardrobe lies:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Now thrive the armourers, and honour’s thought

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Reigns solely in the breast of every man:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: They sell the pasture now to buy the horse,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Following the mirror of all Christian kings,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With winged heels, as English Mercuries.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: For now sits Expectation in the air,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And hides a sword from hilts unto the point

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With crowns imperial, crowns and coronets,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Promised to Harry and his followers.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The French, advised by good intelligence

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Of this most dreadful preparation,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Shake in their fear and with pale policy

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Seek to divert the English purposes.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: O England! model to thy inward greatness,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Like little body with a mighty heart,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: What mightst thou do, that honour would thee do,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Were all thy children kind and natural!

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: But see thy fault! France hath in thee found out

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: A nest of hollow bosoms, which he fills

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: With treacherous crowns; and three corrupted men,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: One, Richard Earl of Cambridge, and the second,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Henry Lord Scroop of Masham, and the third,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Sir Thomas Grey, knight, of Northumberland,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Have, for the gilt of France—O guilt indeed!—

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Confirm’d conspiracy with fearful France;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And by their hands this grace of kings must die,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: If hell and treason hold their promises,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Ere he take ship for France, and in Southampton.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Linger your patience on; and we’ll digest

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The abuse of distance; force a play:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The sum is paid; the traitors are agreed;

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: The king is set from London; and the scene

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Is now transported, gentles, to Southampton.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: There is the playhouse now, there must you sit:

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And thence to France shall we convey you safe,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: And bring you back, charming the narrow seas

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: To give you gentle pass; for, if we may,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: We’ll not offend one stomach with our play.

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: But, till the king come forth, and not till then,

lines.speaker: CHORUS
lines.text: Unto Southampton do we shift our scene.

glosses.phrase: youth of England are on fire
glosses.definition: the young men of England are aflame with martial enthusiasm
soft, idle love-making — silken courtly pleasure
makers and repairers of armour — the war-industry booms

glosses.phrase: sell the pasture now to buy the horse
glosses.definition: sell farmland to buy warhorses — economic re-tooling for war
the model of Christian kingship — Henry as the era's royal paragon
winged feet — the attribute of Mercury, the Roman messenger god
Englishmen imagined as Mercury — swift and divinely errand-bound
personified anticipation — the air of coming battle
the three ranks of royal headgear — imperial crowns, kingly crowns, noble coronets
cowardly statecraft — 'pale' = fearful

glosses.phrase: Like little body with a mighty heart
glosses.definition: small in body but great in spirit — the emerging myth of England's scale
a colony of hollow (treacherous) hearts — the traitors
French gold coins paid as bribes — 'crowns' puns on coins and kingly heads
the first traitor, Richard of Conisburgh — historically motivated by the Mortimer claim, not the French bribe the play emphasizes
the second traitor — Henry's former intimate
the third traitor — a Northumberland knight
France's gold — with a pun on 'guilt' in the next line
Henry, the ornament of kings
arrange; compose
the stage-trick of violating real geography
departed from London
the theatrical space — now imaginatively relocated to Southampton
magically calming the Channel — a theatrical spell
cause any seasickness — the Chorus jokes about the stage's imagined Channel crossing
change the location of the action

annotations.title: The economy of war — England retooling
annotations.body: The Chorus's opening economic image — selling the pasture to buy the horse — is one of the play's most materially specific lines. It captures the Tudor understanding of an expeditionary war: livestock and arable land converted to cavalry; armourers prospering; silk and leisure ('silken dalliance') stored away; the young men mobilized. The Chorus frames this as moral elevation (honour 'reigns solely in the breast of every man'), but the economic mechanism remains visible. 'Now thrive the armourers' is not incidental — Shakespeare is telling us that war is industry. The speech's buoyant tone contrasts sharply with the tavern scene that follows (2.1), in which the war's actual recruits are low-life quarrellers preparing to go pillage for a living. The juxtaposition is deliberate. The Chorus speaks for official England; the play gives us the other England in counterpoint.
sources: Andrew Gurr (ed.), King Henry V (New Cambridge Shakespeare, 1992)
Thomas Healy, Shakespeare, Alienation and the Early Modern Stage (1994)

annotations.title: The three traitors of Southampton
annotations.body: The Chorus names the Southampton plotters — Richard Earl of Cambridge, Henry Lord Scroop of Masham, and Sir Thomas Grey of Northumberland — and attributes their treason simply to French gold. Historically, the situation was more complicated. Cambridge's real motive was dynastic: he was married to the Mortimer line, whose claim to the English throne was arguably stronger than the Lancastrian Henry's. His plot aimed to put his brother-in-law, Edmund Mortimer, Earl of March, on the throne. The play suppresses this because it would introduce the Lancastrian usurpation problem that Henry V itself wants to keep offstage. By reducing the motive to 'the gilt of France,' the Chorus and the later scene collapse a dynastic conspiracy into a simple bribery narrative. This is a characteristic move: the play manages the awkward question of Henry's legitimacy throughout, and the Chorus's simplification is one of its key instruments.
sources: Gary Taylor (ed.), The Oxford Shakespeare: Henry V (1982)
Anne Curry, Agincourt: A New History (2005)

trials.prompt: What does 'They sell the pasture now to buy the horse' (line 5) describe?
options: The economic mobilization of the English countryside for war — selling livestock-land to equip cavalry
The purchase of racehorses for Agincourt
A French trade embargo on English wool

trials.prompt: The Chorus attributes the traitors' treason to 'the gilt of France.' What does the play omit?
options: Nothing — the account is historically complete
That Cambridge's actual motive was dynastic: his Mortimer marriage gave his brother-in-law a rival claim to Henry's throne. The play simplifies the plot into bribery
That one of the traitors was secretly Welsh
That the plot also involved the Dauphin personally

trials.prompt: Why does the Chorus's 'silken dalliance in the wardrobe lies' image matter?
options: It registers the cultural cost of mobilization — ease, pleasure, and courtly idleness are stored away while the war industry thrives
It refers to a ceremonial robe worn at Henry's coronation
